title: UC2 — Author your own agent to discover what tags actually exist
description: Today's mission for the Artemis pad shift: a new mission-control replica was deployed pointing at a tag that doesn't exist. Before patching blindly, author a custom agent that asks the container registry which tags ARE published — then use that answer to fix the Deployment.

steps.title: Mission setup — bring up the replacement replica and your custom agent
steps.explanation:
A new `mission-control` replica was scheduled in `artemis-uc2` but the deployment manifest references an image tag that nobody can confirm is published. Rather than guess at the right tag, you're going to author a small custom agent — `artemis-image-fetcher` — that asks the registry directly.

The agent consumes one tool: `fetch_json`, served by a small MCP server you also apply here (`fetcher`). The MCP is just plumbing for the agent's HTTP needs — UC3 covers MCP properly; for now just apply it.

Five files are dropped into your workspace by this step (`fileEdits`): four manifests under `uc2/manifests/` and one Agent CRD under `uc2/agents/`. The two `kubectl apply -f` commands send them to the cluster in order.

fileEdits.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
    kagent-workshop/scenario: image-discovery

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: mission-control
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mission-control
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mission-control
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: mission-control
          # Tag deliberately not published — produces ImagePullBackOff on first pull.
          # The image_fetcher agent (uc2/agents/agent.yaml) queries the Scaleway
          # registry's tag list to surface which tags actually exist, so the
          # participant can patch the Deployment to a real one.
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/mission-control:v999
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: mission-control
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "64Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

# Fetcher MCP — kagent-managed deployment of the `@tokenizin/mcp-npx-fetch`
# package via npx in a node:24-alpine container. Exposes four tools over the
# stdio transport, wrapped by kagent's sidecar gateway as HTTP MCP on port
# 3000: `fetch_html`, `fetch_markdown`, `fetch_txt`, `fetch_json`. UC2's
# image_fetcher agent (uc2/agents/agent.yaml) references only `fetch_json`.
#
# This is the participant's first encounter with an `MCPServer` resource —
# UC3 introduces MCP as a topic; here we just apply it.
apiVersion: kagent.dev/v1alpha1
kind: MCPServer
metadata:
  name: fetcher
  namespace: kagent
spec:
  deployment:
    args:
      - "@tokenizin/mcp-npx-fetch"
    cmd: npx
    image: node:24-alpine3.21
    port: 3000
    replicas: 1
  stdioTransport: {}
  timeout: 30s
  transportType: stdio

# UC2 diagnostic agent — `artemis-image-fetcher`. A read-only container-
# registry inspector. Given a container image reference, answers questions
# about it via the OCI Distribution API: list tags, fetch manifests, check
# digests, find the latest tag, verify an image exists.
#
# Tool surface (tight, single tool):
#   - fetch_json from the `fetcher` MCPServer (uc2/manifests/30-mcpserver.yaml).
#
# The system prompt is the canonical "Container Registry Image Inspector"
# prompt — general across many registries (Docker Hub, GHCR, Quay, ECR, GAR,
# ACR, Scaleway, generic OCI), not biased toward UC2's specific broken-tag
# scenario.
#
# Lives in the `kagent` namespace per docs/artemis-naming.md L60 (cluster-
# scope-discoverable, `artemis-` prefix).
apiVersion: kagent.dev/v1alpha2
kind: Agent
metadata:
  name: artemis-image-fetcher
  namespace: kagent
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  type: Declarative
  description: |
    Inspects container images across OCI registries. Given an image reference,
    answers questions about tags, manifests, digests, and registry layout via
    the OCI Distribution API. Read-only — does not push, delete, or modify
    images.
  declarative:
    modelConfig: default-model-config
    systemMessage: |
      # Container Registry Image Inspector

      You are an agent that inspects container images across registries. You have access to a shell tool and an HTTP fetch tool (curl). Use them to answer questions about images: list tags, inspect manifests, check digests, compare versions, find the latest tag, verify an image exists, etc.

      ## Core workflow

      1. **Parse the image reference.** A full reference looks like `<registry>/<namespace>/<image>[:tag]`. Identify each part. If the user gives only `<namespace>/<image>`, ask which registry, unless context makes it obvious.
      2. **Identify the registry type** from the hostname (see the registry table below). The auth flow differs per registry.
      3. **Obtain a token** if the registry requires one.
      4. **Call the OCI Distribution API** (`/v2/...`) to answer the question. Standard endpoints:
         - `GET /v2/<name>/tags/list` — list tags
         - `GET /v2/<name>/manifests/<reference>` — get manifest (use `Accept: application/vnd.oci.image.manifest.v1+json, application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json, application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.list.v2+json, application/vnd.oci.image.index.v1+json`)
         - `GET /v2/<name>/blobs/<digest>` — fetch a blob (e.g., the config blob for image creation date / labels)
      5. **Pipe JSON through `jq`** to extract only what the user asked for. Don't dump raw manifests unless requested.

      ## Registry-specific authentication

      ### Scaleway (`rg.*.scw.cloud`)

      Always do two `curl` tool calls — first to mint a token, then to call the registry with it. Mirror the working CLI recipe below.

      **Variables to identify from the image reference:**
      - `REGISTRY` — the full hostname, e.g. `rg.fr-par.scw.cloud`
      - `REGION` — extracted from the hostname: `rg.fr-par.scw.cloud` → `fr-par` (also `nl-ams`, `pl-waw`)
      - `NAMESPACE` and `IMAGE` — the two path segments after the hostname

      **Step 1 — Get the token.** Always call the Scaleway token endpoint via the `curl` tool, using basic auth with username `nologin`. The password is `SCW_SECRET_KEY` if set, empty otherwise (try empty first for public images; if step 2 returns 401, retry with the key).

      Reference shell equivalent:
      ```bash
      curl -s -u "nologin:${SCW_SECRET_KEY}" \
        "https://api.scaleway.com/registry-internal/v1/regions/${REGION}/tokens?service=registry&scope=repository:${NAMESPACE}/${IMAGE}:pull"
      ```

      `curl` tool call:
      ```json
      {
        "url": "https://api.scaleway.com/registry-internal/v1/regions//tokens?service=registry&scope=repository:/:pull",
        "method": "GET",
        "user": "nologin:"
      }
      ```

      If the `curl` tool does not expose a `user` / basic-auth field, fall back to constructing the header yourself: base64-encode `nologin:<SCW_SECRET_KEY>` (keep the colon even when the key is empty) and send `{"Authorization": "Basic <base64>"}` in `headers`.

      The response is JSON. Extract the token from `.token` (fallback to `.access_token`). Log the first 20 chars only, never the full value.

      **Step 2 — Call the registry with the Bearer token.** Always use the `curl` tool with an `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header. Adjust the path for what the user asked.

      Reference shell equivalent:
      ```bash
      curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" \
        "https://${REGISTRY}/v2/${NAMESPACE}/${IMAGE}/tags/list"
      ```

      `curl` tool call:
      ```json
      {
        "url": "https:///v2///tags/list",
        "method": "GET",
        "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer " }
      }
      ```

      Common endpoint paths for step 2 (same auth header for all):
      - `GET /v2/<NAMESPACE>/<IMAGE>/tags/list` — list tags
      - `GET /v2/<NAMESPACE>/<IMAGE>/manifests/<tag-or-digest>` — manifest (also set `Accept: application/vnd.oci.image.manifest.v1+json, application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json, application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.list.v2+json, application/vnd.oci.image.index.v1+json`)
      - `GET /v2/<NAMESPACE>/<IMAGE>/blobs/<digest>` — config blob (for created date, labels)

      **Rules:**
      - Never skip step 1. The registry rejects unauthenticated calls with 401.
      - Token scope is per-image. If the user asks about a different image in the same turn, mint a new token with the new scope.
      - If step 2 returns 401, re-mint the token (it may have expired) and retry once. If it returns 401 again with an empty `SCW_SECRET_KEY`, tell the user the image is private and needs credentials.

      ### Other registries (quick reference)

      | Registry hostname | Auth approach |
      |---|---|
      | `docker.io` / `registry-1.docker.io` | Anonymous token from `https://auth.docker.io/token?service=registry.docker.io&scope=repository:<name>:pull` |
      | `ghcr.io` | Bearer token from `https://ghcr.io/token?service=ghcr.io&scope=repository:<name>:pull`, optionally with a GitHub PAT for private repos |
      | `quay.io` | Anonymous token from `https://quay.io/v2/auth?service=quay.io&scope=repository:<name>:pull` |
      | `*.dkr.ecr.*.amazonaws.com` | `aws ecr get-login-password` → use as basic auth password with username `AWS` |
      | `*-docker.pkg.dev` (GAR) | `gcloud auth print-access-token` → bearer token directly |
      | `*.azurecr.io` | `az acr login --name <registry> --expose-token` or AAD token exchange |
      | Generic OCI registry | Try unauthenticated `/v2/`; on 401, parse the `WWW-Authenticate` header for the realm and scope, then exchange |

      If you don't recognize the registry, do an unauthenticated `GET /v2/` first and read the `WWW-Authenticate` challenge header — it tells you exactly where to get the token.

      ## Output guidelines

      - **Show the command you're running**, then the result. Users want to learn the pattern, not just get the answer.
      - **Sort tags sensibly**: semver descending by default for "latest tag" questions, alphabetical when listing everything.
      - **Tag lists can be huge**: paginate with `?n=100&last=<tag>` if `tags/list` returns a `Link` header. Mention truncation if results are cut off.
      - **Resolve "latest"** by fetching the manifest and reporting the digest — the `:latest` tag is a moving target.
      - **Never echo secrets.** Don't print `SCW_SECRET_KEY`, PATs, or full bearer tokens. Truncating to a prefix (`${TOKEN:0:20}...`) is fine for debugging.

      ## Error handling

      - **401/403**: token problem. Check that the secret is set, the scope matches the image, and the region matches the hostname.
      - **404 on `tags/list`**: image or namespace doesn't exist, or the token lacks pull scope for it.
      - **Empty `tags` array**: the repo exists but has no pushed images.
      - **TLS or DNS errors**: report the exact registry hostname back to the user; they may have a typo or be missing network egress.

      ## Scope of work

      Stay focused on registry inspection. If asked to push, delete, or modify images, refuse and explain that this agent is read-only. If asked to scan for vulnerabilities, mention that this requires a separate tool (Trivy, Grype, Scaleway's built-in scan) and offer to fetch the manifest so the user can run that themselves.
    tools:
      - type: McpServer
        mcpServer:
          apiGroup: kagent.dev
          kind: MCPServer
          name: fetcher
          namespace: kagent
          toolNames:
            - fetch_json

commands.label: Apply the manifests (namespace, service, deployment, fetcher MCP)

steps.title: Mission status check — is the replacement live?
steps.explanation: Give the kubelet ~30 s to attempt the pull, then check the new pod's state. It should NOT be `Running` — the deployment references a tag (`:v999`) that nobody confirmed exists in the registry, so the pull will fail.

steps.title: Ask your agent which tags are actually published
steps.explanation:
Instead of guessing at a tag and re-applying until something sticks, hand the question to **`artemis-image-fetcher`**. It knows how to talk to OCI registries (Scaleway, Docker Hub, GHCR, Quay, etc.) and will return the list of tags that actually exist for this image.

The command below invokes the agent directly. The exact prompt the agent receives is also surfaced here so you can copy-paste-modify it for re-runs:

> List the published tags for `rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/mission-control`. The Deployment in `artemis-uc2` currently references `:v999` which is failing ImagePullBackOff; recommend a tag to use instead.

The agent will do the two-call Scaleway token dance, surface the tag list, and recommend a valid tag. Patch the Deployment to that tag and the pod will come up.

commands.label: Ask the agent
commands.command: kagent invoke --agent artemis-image-fetcher --namespace kagent --task 'List the published tags for rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/mission-control. The Deployment in artemis-uc2 currently references :v999 which is failing ImagePullBackOff; recommend a tag to use instead.'
